item.title: 拥抱异议：在反对观点中寻找价值 | Embrace the Dissent: Finding Value in Opposing Views
item.description:
第三季度伊始，埃隆·马斯克又掀起爆炸性惊喜： Twitter 对用户一天可以阅读的推文数量实施临时限制

如果你快速浏览一下社交媒体格局，人们的反应就像一颗原子弹，可以摧毁 Twitter

然而，在加入战斗口号之前，请简单考虑一下这个问题：

在所有 Twitter 用户中，您认为谁最不可能希望 Twitter 关闭？

为什么？

似乎所有的注意力立刻就被吸引到了极限部分，很少有人谈论马斯克为什么这么做，这是开场白：

解决极端水平的数据抓取和系统操纵问题

尽管尚不完全清楚马斯克所说的“数据抓取”是什么意思，但考虑到最近围绕 chatGPT 生成的人工智能的炒作，该公司很有可能使用数据抓取来训练他们的 LLM（大型语言模型），例如 OpenAI，微软、谷歌和其他公司。

包括我在内的一些人已经受到 Twitter 上的问题的影响。


如果没有施加限制，也许会有更多的人看到它。 因此，请考虑这种限制实际上可能是为了最大数量的人的最大利益。

有人猜测，这一变化的真正原因是通过鼓励人们订阅 Twitter Blue（其付费订阅服务）来创造更多收入。 不过，除非马斯克亲自证实，否则这一点无法得到证实。 即使这是真的，考虑到马斯克在多次采访中描述的财务状况，如果 Twitter 不能很快盈利，它实际上可能会消亡。

算法会让人们更难看到不同的观点

目前，几乎所有流行的社交媒体平台，例如Facebook、Twitter、TikTok和Youtube，都采用了个性化推荐算法。 事实上，即使是Google、Bing等搜索引擎的搜索结果也是个性化的。 虽然这种个性化旨在增强用户体验，但它无意中强化了我们现有的信念和偏好。 结果，我们经常发现自己被志同道合的人包围，被孤立在回音室里，与我们自己的观点和观点相呼应。

这种效应因人类的确认偏见（寻求证实我们现有信念的信息的倾向）而进一步放大。 算法为我们提供符合我们偏好的内容，强化我们的偏见并导致对现实的扭曲理解。 当我们更加固守自己的世界观时，这无意中加剧了社会内部的分裂和两极分化。 一个极端的例子是，全明星篮球运动员凯里·欧文曾公开表示，他相信地球是平的，因为YouTube的兔子洞：

它每天都在软件开发世界中发生

作为软件工程师，我们应该知道没有一种放之四海而皆准的方法，而且总是需要做出权衡。 然而，不断地看到不同框架阵营之间的斗争让我意识到，根本原因在于更深层次的人性。

前端框架中讨论最广泛的话题是React 、 Vue和Angular之间的比较。 我看到一些社交媒体评论批评Vue的作者Even You为了推广Vue而频繁批评其他框架。 然而，在看到他个人账户上的一条推文后，我发现自己很难保持这种观点。 由于是中文写的，所以我逐字翻译成英文：

我从来没有坚持别人必须承认某个方向是更正确或更先进的——事实上，这正是我所反对的。 因为自从我开始使用 Vue 的第一天起，就有人试图将他们的观点强加给我，要求我承认 Angular 或 React 更“正确”。

我可以理解这种情绪，因为我在构建自己的工具包ZenStack时经常遇到这种情况。 它在 Prisma 之上使用模式优先的方法来简化 Web 应用程序开发。 然而，反模式阵营中的一些人立即驳回了模式或 Prisma 的想法，甚至没有考虑它们可以解决的问题。 事实上，最初，我意识到模式的潜在缺点，试图提倡采用非模式方法。 然而，我很快意识到，如果没有模式，解决访问控制的主要问题对用户来说将变得非常困难。 这就是我们所做的权衡。

我们对于它可以做些什么呢？

我喜欢数据科学家 Cathy O'Neil 在 Netflix 纪录片《社会困境》结尾处解决这个问题的方式：

我在 Twitter 上关注我不同意的人，因为我想接触不同的观点

我发现这是培养批判性思维技能和鼓励独立思考的实用方法。

此外，我还发展了自己的一套理论，以提醒人们倾听不同的观点：

我们都是正常人，我并不比其他人聪明。 如果有人持有与我不同的观点，则意味着他们可能拥有我不知道的信息。 我想知道它是什么。

最后，我最喜欢查理·芒格的一句话：

我从不允许自己对任何我不比他们更了解对方论点的事情持有意见

如果您喜欢使用模式作为后端的单一事实来源的想法，那么您绝对应该检查一下； 如果你是反图式阵营，为什么不以此作为倾听不同观点的实践呢？ 😉 😉

github.com/zenstackhq/zenstack github.com/zenstackhq/zenstack



原文:

Elon Musk set off another explosive surprise as Q3 kicked off: Twitter has applied a temporary limit to the number of tweets users can read in a day

If you take a quick look at the social media landscape, people's reactions seem like an atomic bomb that could destroy Twitter

However, before joining the rallying cry, simply consider this question:

Out of all Twitter users, who do you think would be the least likely to want Twitter to shut down?

Why is it?

It seems all the attention is immediately drawn to the limits part, few people are talking about why Musk did so, which is the start sentence:

To address extreme levels of data scraping and system manipulation

Although it is not entirely clear what Musk means by "data scraping", given the recent hype around AI generated by chatGPT, there is a good chance that the company uses data scraping to train their LLMs (large language models), such as OpenAI, Microsoft, Google, and others.

Several individuals, myself included, have already been impacted by the issues on Twitter.


Perhaps more people would have seen it if the limitation had not been applied. Therefore, consider that this limitation may actually be for the greatest good for the greatest number.

There has been speculation that the real reason for this change is to generate more revenue by encouraging people to subscribe to Twitter Blue, its paid subscription service. However, this cannot be verified unless Musk confirms it himself. Even if it is true, given Musk's description of their financial status in many interviews, Twitter might actually die if it cannot generate profit soon.

Algorithms can make it harder to see different points of view

In the present era, nearly all popular social media platforms, such as Facebook, Twitter, TikTok, and Youtube, employ personalized recommendation algorithms. In fact, even the search result of search engines like Google and Bing are personalized. While this personalization aims to enhance user experience, it unintentionally reinforces our existing beliefs and preferences. As a result, we often find ourselves surrounded by like-minded individuals, isolated in echo chambers that echo our own opinions and perspectives.

This effect is further amplified by the confirmation bias of our human beings, the tendency to seek information that confirms our existing beliefs. Algorithms feed us content that aligns with our preferences, reinforcing our biases and leading to a distorted understanding of reality. This inadvertently reinforces the division and polarization within society as we become more entrenched in our own worldviews. One extreme example is that the all-star basketball player Kyrie Irving once publicly stated that he believed the Earth was flat because of the rabbit hole of YouTube:

Kyrie Irving sorry for saying Earth is flat blames it on a YouTube ‘rabbit hole’

It happens in the software development world every day

As software engineers, we are supposed to know that there is no one-size-fits-all approach and that it always involves making trade-offs. However, keep seeing battles between different framework camps makes me realize that the underlying cause lies deeper within human nature.

The most widely discussed topic among frontend frameworks is the comparison between React, Vue, and Angular. I have seen some social media comments that criticized Even You, the author of Vue, for frequently criticizing other frameworks in order to promote Vue. However, I find it difficult to maintain that perspective myself after coming across a single tweet from his personal account. It was written in Chinese, so I translated it into English literally:

I have never insisted that others must acknowledge a certain direction as being more correct or advanced - in fact, this is exactly what I oppose. Because since the first day I started working with Vue, there have been people trying to impose their views on me, demanding that I admit Angular or React as being more "correct.”

I can empathize with this sentiment because I often encounter it while building my own toolkit, ZenStack. It uses a schema-first approach on top of Prisma to simplify web application development. However, some people in the anti-schema camp immediately dismiss the idea of schema or Prisma, without even considering the problems they could solve. In fact, initially, I tried to advocate for a non-schema approach, aware of the potential drawbacks of schema. However, I soon realized that without a schema, solving the major problem of access control would become very difficult for users. So that’s the trade-off we made.

What can we do about it?

I liked how data scientist Cathy O'Neil addressed this issue at the end of Netflix's documentary, The Social Dilemma:

I follow people on Twitter that I disagree with because I want to be exposed to different points of view

I found it to be a practical way to foster critical thinking skills and encourage independent thought.

Additionally, I have developed my own set of theories that serve as a reminder to listen to the different viewpoints:

We are both normal human beings, and I am not inherently smarter than anyone else. If someone holds a different point of view from mine, it means they likely have information that I don't know. I would like to know what it is.

Lastly, there is my favorite quote from Charlie Munger:

I never allow myself to hold an opinion on anything that I don't know the other side's argument better than they do

ZenStack is an open-source toolkit that uses the schema-first approach on top of Prisma ORM to unleash its full potential for full-stack development. If you like the idea of using schema as the single source of truth for your backend, you definitely should check it out; If you are anti-schema camp, why not take this as a practice of listening to the different views? 😉

github.com/zenstackhq/zenstack
item.author: jiasheng

item.title: 简化 Javascript 堆栈中的表单验证 | Streamlining Form Validation in Your Javascript Stack
item.description:
现代 Web 应用程序通常集中处理接受和存储用户生成的数据。 交互可以通过提交表单、上传文件或任何其他形式的交互来进行。 数据验证对于确保应用程序正确、安全地运行至关重要。 然而，在应用程序堆栈的不同位置拥有单独的验证逻辑会使应用程序成本更高且更难以维护。

理想的解决方案是在一个中心位置（靠近定义数据的位置）表达验证规则，并让它在每个应用程序层中生效。 在这篇文章中，我将演示如何使用ZenStack工具包实现这一目标。

场景

让我们使用一个简单的注册页面作为示例。 该页面用于提交个人信息以注册即将举行的聚会：

以下是业务规则：

  • 各个领域都需要。
  • 饮料选择有苏打水、咖啡、小熊啤酒和鸡尾酒。 只有成年提交者才能选择酒精饮料。

我们将使用 Next.js（及其新的“应用程序路由器”目录结构）构建应用程序。

运行以下命令创建项目，并在出现提示时选择使用 TypeScript、TailwindCSS 和 App Router：

 npx create-next-app@latest

我们将使用 ZenStack 与数据库进行数据验证对话。 在新创建的项目目录中运行以下命令来初始化项目以使用 ZenStack：

它应该安装一些依赖项并在项目的根目录下生成一个“schema.zmodel”文件。 现在我们已经完成了项目的搭建并准备添加功能。

数据建模

让我们采用自下而上的方法，从数据库层开始，向上移动到 UI。 如果您熟悉 Prisma，您会发现自己很熟悉，但如果不熟悉也不必担心，因为语法简单直观。

将“/schema.zmodel”的内容更改为以下内容：

 generator client { provider = "prisma-client-js" } datasource db { provider = 'sqlite' url = 'file:./dev.db' } model Signup { id Int @id @default(autoincrement()) name String email String @email @endsWith("@zenstack.dev", 'Must be a @zenstack.dev email') adult Boolean beverage String createdAt DateTime @default(now()) updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt @@allow('create,read', true) // sqlite doesn't support enum, you should use enum in a real application @@validate(beverage in ['SODA', 'COFFEE', 'BEER', 'COCKTAIL'], 'Please choose a valid beverage') @@validate(adult || beverage in ['SODA', 'COFFEE'], 'You must be an adult to drink alcohol') }

一些快速说明：

  • 我们使用@email和@endsWith等属性将验证规则附加到字段。
  • 我们还使用模型级属性@@validate来表达涉及多个字段和条件的验证规则。
  • 默认情况下，所有操作都会被拒绝。 访问控制不是本文的重点，但如果有兴趣，您可以在这里了解更多信息。

运行以下命令生成 Prisma schema 并将其同步到数据库：

 npx zenstack generate npx prisma db push

实施后端

在构建 UI 之前，让我们实现接受注册提交的 API。 使用以下内容创建文件“/src/app/api/signup/route.ts”：

 import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client'; import { withPresets } from '@zenstackhq/runtime'; import { NextResponse } from 'next/server'; // create a database client enhanced by ZenStack that enforces data validation const db = withPresets(new PrismaClient()); export async function POST(request: Request) { const data = await request.json(); try { const result = await db.signup.create({ data }); return NextResponse.json(result, { status: 201 }); } catch (err: any) { return NextResponse.json({ error: err.message }, { status: 400 }); } }

实现相当简单，下面这行是关键：

 const db = withPresets(new PrismaClient());

我们创建了一个 PrismaClient 并使用 ZenStack 包装器“增强”了它，该包装器在运行时注入数据行为逻辑。

建立表格

将文件“/src/app/page.tsx”替换为此处的内容。 下面的代码是 if 的缩写副本。 如果您只是阅读而不是遵循构建过程，您可以检查它：

 'use client'; import { ReactNode, useState } from 'react'; import { FieldError, useForm } from 'react-hook-form'; function FormRow({ label, children, error, }: { name: string; label: string; error?: FieldError; children: ReactNode; }) { return ( <div className="w-full"> <div className="flex gap-4 items-center"> <label className="w-32">{label}</label> <div className="flex-grow">{children}</div> </div> {error && ( <p className="text-red-600 text-sm text-right pt-1"> {error.message} </p> )} </div> ); } interface Input { name: string; email: string; adult: boolean; beverage: string; } export default function Signup() { const { register, handleSubmit, formState: { errors }, } = useForm<Input>({ defaultValues: { beverage: '' }, }); const [serverError, setServerError] = useState(''); async function onSubmit(data: Input) { const resp = await fetch('/api/signup', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify(data), headers: { 'content-type': 'application/json' }, }); if (resp.status !== 201) { setServerError(await resp.text()); } else { alert('Thank you for signing up!'); } } return ( <main className="flex min-h-screen flex-col items-center justify-center p-24"> <div className="container max-w-lg items-center justify-center font-mono flex flex-col"> <h1 className="text-3xl font-semibold mb-16"> 🎉 Join the Party 🥳 </h1> <form className="flex flex-col w-full text-lg gap-6" onSubmit={handleSubmit(onSubmit)} > <FormRow name="name" label="Name" error={errors.name}> <input type="text" placeholder="You name" className={`input input-bordered w-full ${ errors.name ? 'input-error' : '' }`} {...register('name', { required: true, })} /> </FormRow> {/* code of other rows abbreviated, find full content at: https://github.com/ymc9/zenstack-form-validation/blob/backend-only-validation/src/app/page.tsx */} <input className="btn btn-outline" type="submit" /> {serverError && ( <p className="text-red-600 text-sm">{serverError}</p> )} </form> </div> </main> ); }

一些注意事项：

  • 我们使用 DaisyUI 进行简单的样式设计。 您可以在此处找到安装指南。
  • 我们使用react-hooks-form来构建具有简单状态绑定的表单。 使用npm install react-hook-form安装它。 您可以在其网站上找到更多信息。

让我们运行该应用程序，看看如果我们提交违反规则的内容会发生什么。

发生的情况如下：

由于我们没有实现任何客户端检查，因此数据直接发布到服务器 API。 幸运的是，我们的 API 成功拒绝了该请求，因为它使用启用了数据验证的 PrismaClient。 然而，这种体验并不理想，原因有二：

  1. 需要网络往返才能获取错误，这可能很慢。
  2. 如果可能，需要对错误进行解码并附加到该字段。

客户端验证

客户端验证在将表单发布到服务器之前对其进行检查。 使用react-hooks-form 的好处之一是它允许您向字段添加验证规则。 然而，我们实际上并不需要这样做，因为我们已经在 ZModel 中表达了所有规则，并且我们希望重用它！

还记得我们运行了zenstack generate命令将 ZModel 编译为“schema.prisma”吗？ 它具有可扩展的架构，并允许通过插件生成不同类型的工件。 当检测到 ZModel 中的数据验证规则时，内置的“zod”插件会自动启用。 幸运的是，react-hooks-form 有一个附加包，允许您配置用于表单验证的 zod 模式。 让我们看看如何连接它们：

  1. 安装附加包

    npm i @hookform/resolvers

  2. 将导入添加到“/src/app/page.tsx”

     import { SignupCreateSchema } from '@zenstackhq/runtime/zod/models'; import { zodResolver } from '@hookform/resolvers/zod';

  3. 初始化react-hooks-form时设置zod解析器

    const { register, handleSubmit, formState: { errors }, } = useForm<Input>({ defaultValues: { beverage: '' }, resolver: zodResolver(SignupCreateSchema), // <-- add this });

就是这样！ 很简单，对吧？

现在，如果您使用相同的数据再次提交，您将在电子邮件字段中收到一条附加的错误消息：

但是等等，我们的“除非你是成年人否则禁止饮酒”错误去哪儿了？

由于规则是在模型级别（而不是字段级别）表达的，因此当违反规则时，错误不会附加到任何字段，而是保留在空键下的“根”处。

 model Signup { ... @@validate(adult || beverage in [SODA, COFFEE], 'Must be an adult to drink alcohol') }

不过，解决办法很简单； 只需提取它并单独显示：

 const toplevelError = (errors as FieldErrors)['']?.message?.toString(); <form> ... {toplevelError && ( <p className="text-red-600 text-sm">{toplevelError}</p> )} </form>

现在完美了！

结论

我希望您喜欢阅读并发现该方法很有趣。 您可以在github.com/ymc9/zenstack-form-validation找到完整的项目代码。

我们构建了 ZenStack 工具包，相信强大的架构可以带来许多好处，从而简化全栈应用程序的构建。 同构数据验证是一个很好的例子，但还有更多。 查看我们的GitHub 页面了解更多详细信息！

人们说，如果你以猫模因结束帖子，你可以让你的读者做任何事情。 但我是个爱狗的人，所以你走吧......

如果您喜欢 ZenStack 背后的想法，如果您能给它一颗星，以便更多的人可以找到它并从中受益，我将不胜感激！

github.com/zenstackhq/zenstack github.com/zenstackhq/zenstack



原文:

Modern web applications often intensively deal with accepting and storing user-generated data. The interaction can happen by submitting forms, uploading files, or any other form of interactivity. Data validation is crucial to ensuring that the application functions correctly and securely. However, having separate validation logic in different places in the application stack makes the app more costly and harder to maintain.

An ideal solution would be to express the validation rules in a central place (close to where data is defined) and let it take effect in every application layer. In this post, I will demonstrate how to achieve this with the ZenStack toolkit.

The Scenario

Let's use a simple signup page as an example. The page is for submitting personal information to sign up for an upcoming party:

Here're the business rules:

  • All fields are required.
  • The "Email" field must be a valid email ending with "@zenstack.dev".
  • Beverage choices are Soda, Coffee, Bear, and Cocktail. Only adult submitters can choose an alcoholic beverage.

We will build the application using Next.js (with its new "app router" directory structure).

Run the following command to create a project, and when prompted, choose to use TypeScript, TailwindCSS, and App Router:

npx create-next-app@latest

We'll use ZenStack to talk to the database with data validation. ZenStack is built above the awesome Prisma ORM and extends it with a powerful access control and data validation layer. Run the following command in the newly created project directory to initialize your project to use ZenStack:

It should install a few dependencies and generate a "schema.zmodel" file under the project's root. We're now done with scaffolding the project and ready to add features.

Modeling Data

Let's take a bottom-up approach, starting from the database layer and moving upward to the UI. ZenStack provides a modeling language called ZModel to define data schema and validation rules. The ZModel language is based on Prisma's schema language. You'll find yourself home if you're familiar with Prisma, but don't worry if not because the syntax is simple and intuitive.

Change the content of "/schema.zmodel" to the following:

generator client { provider = "prisma-client-js"
} datasource db { provider = 'sqlite' url = 'file:./dev.db'
} model Signup { id Int @id @default(autoincrement()) name String email String @email @endsWith("@zenstack.dev", 'Must be a @zenstack.dev email') adult Boolean beverage String createdAt DateTime @default(now()) updatedAt DateTime @updatedAt @@allow('create,read', true) // sqlite doesn't support enum, you should use enum in a real application @@validate(beverage in ['SODA', 'COFFEE', 'BEER', 'COCKTAIL'], 'Please choose a valid beverage') @@validate(adult || beverage in ['SODA', 'COFFEE'], 'You must be an adult to drink alcohol')
}

A few quick notes:

  • We used attributes like @email and @endsWith to attach validation rules to fields.
  • We also used the model-level attribute @@validate to express validation rules involving multiple fields and conditions.
  • The @@allow attribute grants "create" and "read" access to the table. By default, all operations are denied. Access control is not a focus of this post, but you can learn more here if interested.

Run the following commands to generate a Prisma schema and synchronize it to the database:

npx zenstack generate
npx prisma db push

Implementing The Backend

Before building the UI, let's implement the API that accepts the signup submission. Create the file "/src/app/api/signup/route.ts" with the following content:

import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';
import { withPresets } from '@zenstackhq/runtime';
import { NextResponse } from 'next/server'; // create a database client enhanced by ZenStack that enforces data validation
const db = withPresets(new PrismaClient()); export async function POST(request: Request) { const data = await request.json(); try { const result = await db.signup.create({ data }); return NextResponse.json(result, { status: 201 }); } catch (err: any) { return NextResponse.json({ error: err.message }, { status: 400 }); }
}

The implementation is fairly straightforward, and the following line is the key:

const db = withPresets(new PrismaClient());

We created a PrismaClient and "enhanced" it with a ZenStack wrapper that injects data behavior logic at runtime.

Building The Form

Replace the file "/src/app/page.tsx" with content here. Code below is an abbreviated copy of if. You can check it if you're just reading instead of following the building process:

'use client'; import { ReactNode, useState } from 'react';
import { FieldError, useForm } from 'react-hook-form'; function FormRow({ label, children, error,
}: { name: string; label: string; error?: FieldError; children: ReactNode;
}) { return ( <div className="w-full"> <div className="flex gap-4 items-center"> <label className="w-32">{label}</label> <div className="flex-grow">{children}</div> </div> {error && ( <p className="text-red-600 text-sm text-right pt-1"> {error.message} </p> )} </div> );
} interface Input { name: string; email: string; adult: boolean; beverage: string;
} export default function Signup() { const { register, handleSubmit, formState: { errors }, } = useForm<Input>({ defaultValues: { beverage: '' }, }); const [serverError, setServerError] = useState(''); async function onSubmit(data: Input) { const resp = await fetch('/api/signup', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify(data), headers: { 'content-type': 'application/json' }, }); if (resp.status !== 201) { setServerError(await resp.text()); } else { alert('Thank you for signing up!'); } } return ( <main className="flex min-h-screen flex-col items-center justify-center p-24"> <div className="container max-w-lg items-center justify-center font-mono flex flex-col"> <h1 className="text-3xl font-semibold mb-16"> 🎉 Join the Party 🥳 </h1> <form className="flex flex-col w-full text-lg gap-6" onSubmit={handleSubmit(onSubmit)} > <FormRow name="name" label="Name" error={errors.name}> <input type="text" placeholder="You name" className={`input input-bordered w-full ${ errors.name ? 'input-error' : '' }`} {...register('name', { required: true, })} /> </FormRow> {/* code of other rows abbreviated, find full content at: https://github.com/ymc9/zenstack-form-validation/blob/backend-only-validation/src/app/page.tsx */} <input className="btn btn-outline" type="submit" /> {serverError && ( <p className="text-red-600 text-sm">{serverError}</p> )} </form> </div> </main> );
}

A few notes:

  • We're using DaisyUI for simple styling. You can find the installation guide here.
  • We're using react-hooks-form to build the form with easy state binding. Install it with npm install react-hook-form. You can find more information on its website.

Let's run the app and see what happens if we submit something that violates the rules.

Here's what happens:

Since we didn't implement any client-side check, the data is directly posted to the server API. Fortunately, our API managed to reject the request since it uses the data-validation-enabled PrismaClient. However, the experience is not ideal for two reasons:

  1. It takes a network roundtrip to get the error, which can be slow.
  2. The error needs to be decoded and attached to the field when possible.

Client-Side Validation

Client-side validation checks a form before posting it to the server. One benefit of using react-hooks-form is that it allows you to add validation rules to fields. However, we don't really need to do that because we've already expressed all the rules in the ZModel, and we would like to reuse it!

Remember we ran the zenstack generate command to compile the ZModel into "schema.prisma"? The zenstack CLI is more than a Prisma schema compiler. It has an extensible architecture and allows to generate different kinds of artifacts via plugins. A built-in "zod" plugin is automatically enabled when it detects data validation rules in the ZModel. Zod schemas are generated for each model and can be imported from @zenstackhq/runtime/zod.

Luckily, react-hooks-form has an add-on package that allows you to configure a zod schema for form validation. Let's see how to hook them up:

  1. Install the add-on package

    npm i @hookform/resolvers

  2. Add imports to “/src/app/page.tsx”

    import { SignupCreateSchema } from '@zenstackhq/runtime/zod/models';
import { zodResolver } from '@hookform/resolvers/zod';

  3. Set up the zod resolver when initializing react-hooks-form

    const { register, handleSubmit, formState: { errors },
} = useForm<Input>({ defaultValues: { beverage: '' }, resolver: zodResolver(SignupCreateSchema), // <-- add this
});

That’s it! Simple, right?

Now if you submit again with the same data, you'll get a nice error message attached to the Email field:

But wait, where did our "no alcohol unless you're an adult" error go?

Since the rule is expressed at the model level (rather than the field level), when it's violated, the error is not attached to any field but stays at the "root" under an empty key.

model Signup { ... @@validate(adult || beverage in [SODA, COFFEE], 'Must be an adult to drink alcohol')
}

The fix is easy, though; just extract it and show it separately:

const toplevelError = (errors as FieldErrors)['']?.message?.toString(); <form> ... {toplevelError && ( <p className="text-red-600 text-sm">{toplevelError}</p> )}
</form>

Perfect now!

Conclusion

I hope you enjoyed the reading and find the approach interesting. You can find the completed project code at github.com/ymc9/zenstack-form-validation.

We built the ZenStack toolkit believing that a powerful schema can bring many benefits that simplify the construction of a full-stack application. Isomorphic data validation is an excellent example, but there's a lot more than that. Check out our GitHub page for more details!

People say you can make your readers do anything if you end a post with a cat meme. But I'm a dog person, so here you go ...

If you like the ideas behind ZenStack, I'd appreciate it if you could give it a star so more people can find and benefit from it!

github.com/zenstackhq/zenstack
item.author: ymc9

item.title: Power Automate - 动态变量 | Power Automate - Dynamic Variables
item.description:
让我们从动态变量是什么开始，然后解释为什么它如此酷。

动态变量是指我们能够根据另一个变量来选择变量，即动态构造表达式的各个部分。

在 Power Automate 中，如果我们想在 2 个值之间进行选择，可以使用 if() 表达式或条件/开关。 但这有一个缺点，要么是二元选择，要么过于复杂（想象一下有 100 个选项的 Switch）。

但你可以做的是动态选择变量/操作属性，让我举个例子。

假设如果变量 = Integer，则使用 SharePoint Field_Integer，如果它是 String，则获取 SharePoint Field_String。

 if(equals(variable('var'),'Integer'), outputs('Get_items')?['body/value']?['Field_Integer'] , outputs('Get_items')?['body/value']?['Field_String'] )

然而，通过动态变量，我们可以做到这一点

outputs('Get_items')?['body/value']?[concat('Field_',variable('var'))]

因此可以从变量设置任何属性。

outputs('Get_items')?['body/value']?[YOUR VARIABLE]

我主要使用它是当我更新带有大量占位符的 html 模板文件时。 在我执行以下操作之前

replace( replace( replace( replace( replace( replace( replace( replace( replace( replace( replace( replace( replace( body('Get_file_content_template'), '{name}', outputs('Get_item')?['body/Title'] ), '{sdd}', base64ToString(base64(body('HTTP'))) ), '{type}', outputs('Get_item')?['body/App_x002f_Automation/Value'] ), '{business}', outputs('Get_item')?['body/BusinessTeam'] ), '{geo}', outputs('Get_item')?['body/Location/Value'] ), '{solution}', outputs('Get_item')?['body/ShortDescriptionoffunctionality'] ), '{storage}', body('Create_HTML_table_data') ), '{trigger}', if(equals(outputs('Get_item')?['body/Trigger/Value'],null),'',outputs('Get_item')?['body/Trigger/Value']) ), '{connections}', body('Create_HTML_table_con') ), '{privileges}', body('Create_HTML_table_privil') ), '{output}', body('Create_HTML_table_out') ), '{data}', outputs('Get_item')?['body/ShortDescriptionofDataHandled'] ), '{owner}', outputs('Get_item')?['body/Author/Email'] )

要更新以下文件：

虽然效率很高，但阅读起来却是一场噩梦，更不用说维护/更新了。

但是使用动态变量，我可以创建一个引用表，对其进行循环并使用它来更新每个占位符。

占位符是模板中的文本，变量是我想要替换它的属性。

我将模板文档存储在变量中，然后循环遍历列表中的每个项目以替换占位符。 由于您无法自引用变量，因此我首先将其传递给组合，然后将变量设置为组合。


 replace(outputs('Reset_variable'), items('Apply_to_each')?['Title'], outputs('Get_item')?['body']?[items('Apply_to_each')?['Variable']] )

现在，如果每个属性都来自同一个操作（例如 Get_item），则可以正常工作，但如果它们来自多个操作，会发生什么情况。 在这种情况下，我们需要创建一个合并对象，然后传递它。

然后我们引用对象的输出（在本例中是一个组合）：

 replace(outputs('Reset_variable'), items('Apply_to_each')?['Title'], outputs('Object')?[items('Apply_to_each')?['Variable']] )

我必须承认这是一个利基功能（我是代码而不是操作的大力倡导者，所以我上面的例子违背了我自己的原则）。 但在某些情况下，将它放在你的后口袋里会拯救你（例如，SharePoint http 操作的动态列表）。 还有一些额外的灵活性是黄金（想象一下有多个不同的模板不断更新，我们可以过滤占位符列表并为每个模板返回不同的占位符）。

是的，我对酷的想法可能有点“独特”😎

丹妮拉·巴拉扎特 - 7 月 3 日

一旦被暂停，wyattdave 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到其暂停被解除。

一旦解除暂停，wyattdave 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，wyattdave 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己才能访问。

如果 wyattdave 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从仪表板重新发布帖子。

笔记：

感谢您保证 DEV 社区的安全。 您可以执行以下操作来标记 wyattdave：

使 wyattdave 的所有帖子不那么明显

举报其他不当行为

坚持 wyattdave 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Let's start with what a dynamic variable is, then why it's so cool.

A dynamic variable is when we are able to select the variable based on another variable, i.e. dynamically construct the parts of an expression.

In Power Automate if we want to choose between 2 values, we can use either the if() expression, or Conditions/Switches. But this has the drawback of either binary choices, or over complexity (just imagine a Switch with a 100 options).

But what you can do is select a variable/action property dynamically, let me give an example.

Lets say if a variable = Integer then use SharePoint Field_Integer, if it is String then get SharePoint Field_String.

if(equals(variable('var'),'Integer'),
  outputs('Get_items')?['body/value']?['Field_Integer']
,
  outputs('Get_items')?['body/value']?['Field_String']
)

Yet with dynamic variables we could do this

outputs('Get_items')?['body/value']?[concat('Field_',variable('var'))]

So any property can be set from a variable.

outputs('Get_items')?['body/value']?[YOUR VARIABLE]

The main time I use this is when I update a html template file with lots of placeholders. Before I would do the below

replace(
    replace(
        replace(
            replace(
                replace(
                    replace(
                        replace(
                            replace(
                                replace(
                                    replace(
                                        replace(
                                            replace(
                                                replace(
                                                    body('Get_file_content_template'),
                                                    '{name}',
                                                    outputs('Get_item')?['body/Title']
                                                ),
                                                '{sdd}',
                                                base64ToString(base64(body('HTTP')))
                                            ),
                                            '{type}',
                                            outputs('Get_item')?['body/App_x002f_Automation/Value']
                                        ),
                                        '{business}',
                                        outputs('Get_item')?['body/BusinessTeam']
                                    ),
                                    '{geo}',
                                    outputs('Get_item')?['body/Location/Value']
                                ),
                                '{solution}',
                                outputs('Get_item')?['body/ShortDescriptionoffunctionality']
                            ),
                            '{storage}',
                            body('Create_HTML_table_data')
                        ),
                        '{trigger}',
                        if(equals(outputs('Get_item')?['body/Trigger/Value'],null),'',outputs('Get_item')?['body/Trigger/Value'])
                    ),
                    '{connections}',
                    body('Create_HTML_table_con')
                ),
                '{privileges}',
                body('Create_HTML_table_privil')
            ),
            '{output}',
            body('Create_HTML_table_out')
        ),
        '{data}',
        outputs('Get_item')?['body/ShortDescriptionofDataHandled']
    ),
    '{owner}',
    outputs('Get_item')?['body/Author/Email']
)

To update the below file:

Which although efficient, is a nightmare to read let alone maintain/update.

But with dynamic variables I can create a reference table, loop over it and use it to update each placeholder.

The placeholder is the text in the template, the variable is the property I want to replace it with.

I store the template document in a variable, then loop over each item in the list replacing the placeholders. As you can't self reference a variable I pass it to a compose first and then set the variable to the compose.


replace(outputs('Reset_variable'),
    items('Apply_to_each')?['Title'],
    outputs('Get_item')?['body']?[items('Apply_to_each')?['Variable']]
)

Now this works ok if every property is from same action (e.g Get_item), but what happens if they are from multiple. Well in that case we need to create a consolidation object, and then pass that.

We then reference that output from the object (in this case a compose):

replace(outputs('Reset_variable'),
    items('Apply_to_each')?['Title'],
    outputs('Object')?[items('Apply_to_each')?['Variable']]
)

I must admit this is a niche feature (I am a big advocate of code instead of actions, so my above example goes against my own principles). But there will be certain scenarios where having this in your back pocket will rescue you (e.g. a dynamic list for SharePoint http action). And others where the extra flexibility is gold (imagine having multiple different templates continually updated, we can filter the placeholder list and return different placeholders for each).

And yes, my idea of cool maybe a little 'unique' 😎

Daniela Barazarte - Jul 3

AdityaPratapBhuyan - Jul 4

Once suspended, wyattdave will not be able to comment or publish posts until their suspension is removed.

Once unsuspended, wyattdave will be able to comment and publish posts again.

Once unpublished, all posts by wyattdave will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

If wyattdave is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

Note:

Thanks for keeping DEV Community safe. Here is what you can do to flag wyattdave:

Make all posts by wyattdave less visible

wyattdave consistently posts content that violates DEV Community's code of conduct because it is harassing, offensive or spammy.

Report other inappropriate conduct

Unflagging wyattdave will restore default visibility to their posts.
item.author: wyattdave

item.title: Typescript 🟦 CRUD API：Next.js、Tailwind、tRPC、Prisma Postgres、Docker | Typescript 🟦 CRUD API: Next.js, Tailwind, tRPC, Prisma Postgres, Docker
item.description:
弗朗西斯科·丘拉

发布于 7 月 4 日

在本文结束时，您将了解如何使用以下技术构建完整的 CRUD API 应用程序，包括使用它的简单前端：

  • 打字稿
  • 顺风 CSS
  • 远程过程调用
  • 棱镜
  • 码头工人

它们涉及多种技术，但我们将尽可能使示例保持基本，以使其易于理解。 我们还将使用 Zod，一个验证库。

如果您喜欢视频版本：

所有代码均可在GitHub上免费获取（视频描述中的链接）。

让我们开始吧。

以下是我们要创建的应用程序架构的架构：

我们将为基本 CRUD 操作创建五个端点：

创建 读取全部 读取一个 更新

删除

我们将使用 Create T3 App 创建应用程序，并将该应用程序连接到在 docker 容器中运行的 Postgres 实例。

我们将提供分步指南，以便您可以遵循。

步骤如下：

  1. 先决条件
  2. 项目创建和依赖安装
  3. 使用 Docker 运行 Postgres 数据库
  4. 配置 Prisma 和数据库架构
  5. 编写tRPC程序
  6. 在 index.tsx 文件中配置处理程序
  7. 使用 Tailwind 编写简单的前端应用程序

要求：

  • 码头工人
  • 任何编辑器（我将使用 VS Code）

您可以通过键入以下内容来检查 Node 和 Docker 版本：

我建议使用最新版本。

为了创建我们的应用程序，我们将使用“创建 T3 应用程序”。 它是一个 CLI 工具，可以使用所有必要的技术创建新项目。


选择除 nextauth 之外的所有内容（使用空格键选择/取消选择）

这通常需要几分钟。

然后进入目录：

现在使用任何 IDE 打开项目。 如果您使用 VS Code，您可以输入：

该项目应该与此类似。

我们可以通过键入以下内容在本地测试应用程序：

并访问localhost:3000

让我们使用 Docker 运行一个 Postgres 容器。

为此，请在项目的根级别创建一个名为docker-compose.yml的新文件。

然后使用以下代码填充该文件：

 version: "3.9" services: db: container_name: db image: postgres:12 ports: - "5432:5432" environment: - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres - POSTGRES_USER=postgres - POSTGRES_DB=postgres volumes: - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data volumes: pgdata: {}

解释：

  • 在本例中，它是db

  • 我们将使用 Postgres 版本 12

  • 我们将使用默认的 Postgres 以避免混淆。

  • 我们正在使用一个命名卷，我们也在下面定义它。

现在让我们使用以下命令运行该容器：

让我们检查容器是否已启动并正在运行：

现在我们准备将应用程序连接到数据库并创建数据库模式

要将我们的应用程序连接到数据库，请打开.env文件并将内容替换为以下内容：

 DATABASE_URL="postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/postgres"

该文件将添加到 .gitignore，而不是推送到公共存储库，因此如果您克隆该项目，则必须创建该文件。

现在打开并编辑prisma/schema.prisma文件，将其替换为以下内容：

 // This is your Prisma schema file, // learn more about it in the docs: https://pris.ly/d/prisma-schema generator client { provider = "prisma-client-js" previewFeatures = ["jsonProtocol"] } datasource db { provider = "postgresql" url = env("DATABASE_URL") } //User with an id, name and email as strings model User { id String @id @default(uuid()) name String email String }

我们用postgresql替换提供者，用 User 替换模型：

所有字段都是字符串。

现在，要更新数据库中的架构，您可以键入：

 npx prisma migrate dev --name init

为了测试一切是否正常工作，我们可以使用 Prisma Studio，这是 Prisma 附带的工具。 类型：

并打开localhost:5555

然后您可以手动添加一条记录。 这稍后会派上用场。

现在我们已经设置了数据库并连接了我们的应用程序，我们可以开始编写一些代码了。

打开src/server/api/routers/example.ts文件并将内容替换为以下内容：

 import { z } from "zod"; import { createTRPCRouter, publicProcedure } from "~/server/api/trpc"; const idSchema = z.object({ id: z.string() }); const userSchema = z.object({ name: z.string(), email: z.string(), }); const userUpdateSchema = z.object({ id: z.string(), name: z.string(), email: z.string(), }); export const exampleRouter = createTRPCRouter({ //get all users getAll: publicProcedure.query(({ ctx }) => { return ctx.prisma.user.findMany(); }), //get user by id getOne: publicProcedure .input(idSchema) .query(({ input, ctx }) => { return ctx.prisma.user.findUnique({ where: idSchema.parse(input), }); }), //create user createUser: publicProcedure .input(userSchema) .mutation(({ input, ctx }) => { return ctx.prisma.user.create({ data: userSchema.parse(input), }); }), //update user updateUser: publicProcedure .input(userUpdateSchema) .mutation(({ input, ctx }) => { return ctx.prisma.user.update({ where: { id: input.id.toString(), }, data: userUpdateSchema.parse(input), }); }), //delete user deleteUser: publicProcedure .input(idSchema) .mutation(({ input, ctx }) => { return ctx.prisma.user.delete({ where: idSchema.parse(input), }); }), });

解释：

  • 我们将使用它来验证函数的输入和输出。

  • 它需要一个具有我们想要公开的功能的对象。

  • 如需进一步说明，请查看： youtu.be/Gf9RkaHnsR8 ?t=406

    我们只需要再编辑一个文件。

    打开src/pages/index.tsx并使用以下内容填充它：

     import { useState } from "react"; import { api } from "~/utils/api"; export default function Home() { //define constants const [name, setName] = useState(""); const [email, setEmail] = useState(""); const [nameToUpdate, setNameToUpdate] = useState(""); const [emailToUpdate, setEmailToUpdate] = useState(""); const [userId, setUserId] = useState(""); const [userIdToUpdate, setUserIdToUpdate] = useState(""); const [userIdToDelete, setUserIdToDelete] = useState(""); //define functions const fetchAllUsers = api.example.getAll.useQuery(); const fetchOneUser = api.example.getOne.useQuery({ id: userId }); const createUserMutation = api.example.createUser.useMutation(); const updateUserMutation = api.example.updateUser.useMutation(); const deleteUserMutation = api.example.deleteUser.useMutation(); //define handlers const handleCreateUser = async () => { try { await createUserMutation.mutateAsync({ name: name, email: email, }); setName(""); setEmail(""); fetchAllUsers.refetch(); } catch (error) { console.log(error); } }; const handleUpdateUser = async () => { try { await updateUserMutation.mutateAsync({ id: userIdToUpdate, name: nameToUpdate, email: emailToUpdate, }); setNameToUpdate(""); setEmailToUpdate(""); setUserIdToUpdate(""); fetchAllUsers.refetch(); } catch (error) { console.log(error); } }; const handleDeleteUser = async () => { try { await deleteUserMutation.mutateAsync({ id: userIdToDelete, }); setUserIdToDelete(""); fetchAllUsers.refetch(); } catch (error) { console.log(error); } }; //return an empty div return ( <div className="mx-auto p-8"> <div className="mb-8"> <h2 className="mb-4 text-2xl font-bold">Get All Users</h2> </div> <button className="rounded bg-blue-500 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-blue-600" onClick={() => fetchAllUsers.refetch()} > Get All Users </button> <div className="text- mb-4 mt-4 grid grid-cols-3 gap-4 font-bold"> <p>Id</p> <p>Name</p> <p>Email</p> </div> {fetchAllUsers.data && fetchAllUsers.data.map((user) => ( <div key={user.id} className="my-4 grid grid-cols-3 gap-4 rounded border border-gray-300 bg-white p-4 shadow" > <p>{user.id}</p> <p>{user.name}</p> <p>{user.email}</p> </div> ))} {/* Get one user UI */} <div className="mb-8"> <h2 className="mb-4 text-2xl font-bold">Get One User</h2> <div className="mb-4 flex"> <input className="mr-2 border border-gray-300 p-2" placeholder="Enter user id to get" value={userId || ""} onChange={(e) => setUserId(String(e.target.value))} /> <button className="rounded bg-blue-500 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-blue-600" onClick={() => fetchOneUser.refetch()} > Get One User </button> </div> {fetchOneUser.data && ( <div> <p>Name: {fetchOneUser.data.name}</p> <p>Email: {fetchOneUser.data.email}</p> </div> )} </div> {/* Create User */} <div className="mb-8"> <h2 className="mb-4 text-2xl font-bold">Create New User</h2> <div className="mb-4 flex"> <input className="mr-2 w-1/2 border border-gray-300 p-2" placeholder="Name" value={name} onChange={(e) => setName(e.target.value)} /> <input className="w-1/2 border border-gray-300 p-2" placeholder="Email" value={email} onChange={(e) => setEmail(e.target.value)} /> </div> <button className="rounded bg-green-500 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-green-600" onClick={handleCreateUser} > Create User </button> </div> {/* Update User */} <div className="mb-8"> <h2 className="mb-4 text-2xl font-bold">Update User</h2> <div className="mb-4 flex"> <input className="mr-2 w-1/2 border border-gray-300 p-2" placeholder="Name to update" value={nameToUpdate} onChange={(e) => setNameToUpdate(e.target.value)} /> <input className="w-1/2 border border-gray-300 p-2" placeholder="Email to update" value={emailToUpdate} onChange={(e) => setEmailToUpdate(e.target.value)} /> </div> <input placeholder="Enter user id to update" className="mr-2 border border-gray-300 p-2" value={userIdToUpdate} onChange={(e) => setUserIdToUpdate(e.target.value)} /> <button className="mt-2 rounded bg-orange-500 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-orange-600" onClick={handleUpdateUser} > Update User </button> </div> {/* Delete User */} <div className="mb-8"> <h2 className="mb-4 text-2xl font-bold">Delete User</h2> <input placeholder="Enter user id to delete" className="mr-2 border border-gray-300 p-2" value={userIdToDelete} onChange={(e) => setUserIdToDelete(e.target.value)} /> <button className="mt-2 rounded bg-red-500 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-red-600" onClick={handleDeleteUser} > Delete User </button> </div> </div> ); }

    解释：

    • 我们使用 Next.js 创建一个单页面应用程序，使用useRouter挂钩从 URL 获取查询参数。

    • 我们使用react-query中的useMutation钩子来创建、更新和删除用户。

    • 我们使用react-query中的useQuery钩子来获取所有用户并获取一个用户。

    • 我们有五个函数来处理 CRUD 操作：

    • 我们为每个使用 Tailwind CSS 设计的功能创建一个 UI。

      每次操作后，我们重新获取数据以查看更改。

      例如，创建新用户后，我们重新获取所有用户以在列表中看到新用户。

      如需进一步说明，请观看视频： youtu.be/Gf9RkaHnsR8

      我们的最终项目应该如下所示：

      该项目旨在作为示例，因此您可以将其用作项目的起点。 如果您想做出贡献，请随时在GitHub上打开 PR（视频描述中的链接）。

      我们使用以下技术构建了 CRUD API：

      • 打字稿
      • 顺风 CSS
      • 远程过程调用
      • 棱镜
      • 码头工人

      如果您喜欢视频版本：

      所有代码均可在GitHub上免费获取（视频描述中的链接）。

      您可以在这里与弗朗西斯科联系

      一旦被暂停，francescoxx 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到其暂停被解除。

      一旦解除暂停，francescoxx 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

      一旦取消发布，francescoxx 的所有帖子都将被隐藏，只有他们自己才能访问。

      如果 francescoxx 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从仪表板重新发布帖子。

      笔记：

      感谢您保证 DEV 社区的安全。 您可以执行以下操作来标记 francescoxx：

      使 francescoxx 的所有帖子不那么明显

      举报其他不当行为

取消标记 francescoxx 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Francesco Ciulla

Posted on Jul 4

 

By the end of this article, you will understand how to build a FULL CRUD API app, including a simple frontend to use it, using the following technologies:

  • Next.js
  • TypeScript
  • Tailwind CSS
  • tRPC
  • Prisma
  • Postgres
  • Docker

They are MANY technologies, but we'll keep the example as basic as possible to make it understandable. We will also use Zod, a validation library.

If you prefer a video version:

All the code is available for free on GitHub (link in video description).

Let's get started.

🏁 Intro

Here is a schema of the architecture of the application we are going to create:

We will create five endpoints for basic CRUD operations:

Create Read all Read one Update

Delete

We will create the application using Create T3 App, and we will connect this application to a Postgres instance running in a docker container.

👣 Steps

We will go with a step-by-step guide so that you can follow along.

Here are the steps:

  1. Prerequisites
  2. Project creation and dependency installation
  3. Run the Postgres database with Docker
  4. Configure Prisma and the database schema
  5. Write the tRPC procedures
  6. Configure the handlers in the index.tsx file
  7. write the simple frontend App using Tailwind

💡 Prerequisites

Requirements:

  • Node.js
  • Docker
  • Any editor (I'll use VS Code)

You can check Node and Docker versions by typing:

I suggest using the latest versions.

🚀 Create a new project using Create T3 App

To create our app, we will use "Create T3 App". It's a CLI tool to create a new project with all the necessary technologies.


Select all except nextauth (use the spacebar to select/deselect)

This usually takes a couple of minutes.

Then step into the directory:

Now open the project using any IDE. If you use VS Code, you can type:

The project should look similar to this.

We can test the app locally by typing:

And visit localhost:3000

🐳 Run the Postgres container using Docker

Let's run a Postgres container using Docker.

To do this, create a new file called docker-compose.yml at the root level of the project.

Then populate the file with the following code:

version: "3.9" services: db: container_name: db image: postgres:12 ports: - "5432:5432" environment: - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres - POSTGRES_USER=postgres - POSTGRES_DB=postgres volumes: - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data volumes: pgdata: {}

Explanation:

  • db is the name of the single service (container) we will run

  • container_name is the custom name we are using. In this case, it's db

  • image is the image we are using from DockerHub. We will use Postgres version 12

  • ports is the mapping of the external-internal ports for the container. We will use the default Postgres one to avoid confusion.

  • environment is to define the environment variables: we will use "postgres" for the user, password, and the database (Don't do this in production!)

  • volumes is to declare the volumes we want to use in this service. We are using a named volume that we also define below.

Now let's run this container by using the command:

And let's check that the container is up and running:

Now we are ready to connect our application to the database and create the database schema

🔼 Connect the App to the Postgres database using Prisma

To connect our application to the database, open the .env file and replace the content with the following:

DATABASE_URL="postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/postgres"

This file will be added to the .gitignore and not be pushed to the public repository, so if you cloned the project, you would have to create this file.

Now open and edit the prisma/schema.prisma file replacing it with the following:

// This is your Prisma schema file,
// learn more about it in the docs: https://pris.ly/d/prisma-schema

generator client {
    provider = "prisma-client-js"
    previewFeatures = ["jsonProtocol"]
}

datasource db {
    provider = "postgresql"
    url      = env("DATABASE_URL")
}

//User with an id, name and email as strings
model User {
    id    String @id @default(uuid())
    name  String
    email String
}

We replace the provider with postgresql and the model with a User with:

All fields are strings.

Now, to update the schema in the DB, you can type:

npx prisma migrate dev --name init

And to test if everything is working correctly, we can use Prisma Studio, a tool that comes with Prisma. Type:

And open localhost:5555

Then you can add a record manually. This will come in handy later.

__

⌨️ Code the Next.js application

📜 example.ts file

Now that we have set up the database and connected our app, we can start writing some code.

Open the src/server/api/routers/example.ts file and replace the content with the following:

import { z } from "zod";
import { createTRPCRouter, publicProcedure } from "~/server/api/trpc"; const idSchema = z.object({ id: z.string() }); const userSchema = z.object({ name: z.string(), email: z.string(),
}); const userUpdateSchema = z.object({ id: z.string(), name: z.string(), email: z.string(),
}); export const exampleRouter = createTRPCRouter({ //get all users getAll: publicProcedure.query(({ ctx }) => { return ctx.prisma.user.findMany(); }), //get user by id getOne: publicProcedure .input(idSchema) .query(({ input, ctx }) => { return ctx.prisma.user.findUnique({ where: idSchema.parse(input), }); }), //create user createUser: publicProcedure .input(userSchema) .mutation(({ input, ctx }) => { return ctx.prisma.user.create({ data: userSchema.parse(input), }); }), //update user updateUser: publicProcedure .input(userUpdateSchema) .mutation(({ input, ctx }) => { return ctx.prisma.user.update({ where: { id: input.id.toString(), }, data: userUpdateSchema.parse(input), }); }), //delete user deleteUser: publicProcedure .input(idSchema) .mutation(({ input, ctx }) => { return ctx.prisma.user.delete({ where: idSchema.parse(input), }); }),
});

Explanation:

  • z is a library to validate the input and output of the functions. We will use it to validate the input and output of the functions.

  • createTRPCRouter is a function that creates a router for us. It takes an object with the functions we want to expose.

  • publicProcedure is a function that takes a schema and returns a function that takes a function.

  • idSchema is a schema that takes an object with an id as a string.

  • userSchema is a schema that takes an object with a name and an email as strings.

  • userUpdateSchema is a schema that takes an object with an id, a name, and an email as strings.

  • exampleRouter is the router we will use to expose the functions.

  • getAll is a function that returns all the users.

  • getOne is a function that returns one user by id.

  • createUser is a function that creates a user.

  • updateUser is a function that updates a user.

  • deleteUser is a function that deletes a user.

For a further explanation, check: youtu.be/Gf9RkaHnsR8?t=406

📜 index.tsx file

We need to edit just one more file.

Open src/pages/index.tsx and populate it with the following:

import { useState } from "react";
import { api } from "~/utils/api"; export default function Home() { //define constants const [name, setName] = useState(""); const [email, setEmail] = useState(""); const [nameToUpdate, setNameToUpdate] = useState(""); const [emailToUpdate, setEmailToUpdate] = useState(""); const [userId, setUserId] = useState(""); const [userIdToUpdate, setUserIdToUpdate] = useState(""); const [userIdToDelete, setUserIdToDelete] = useState(""); //define functions const fetchAllUsers = api.example.getAll.useQuery(); const fetchOneUser = api.example.getOne.useQuery({ id: userId }); const createUserMutation = api.example.createUser.useMutation(); const updateUserMutation = api.example.updateUser.useMutation(); const deleteUserMutation = api.example.deleteUser.useMutation(); //define handlers const handleCreateUser = async () => { try { await createUserMutation.mutateAsync({ name: name, email: email, }); setName(""); setEmail(""); fetchAllUsers.refetch(); } catch (error) { console.log(error); } }; const handleUpdateUser = async () => { try { await updateUserMutation.mutateAsync({ id: userIdToUpdate, name: nameToUpdate, email: emailToUpdate, }); setNameToUpdate(""); setEmailToUpdate(""); setUserIdToUpdate(""); fetchAllUsers.refetch(); } catch (error) { console.log(error); } }; const handleDeleteUser = async () => { try { await deleteUserMutation.mutateAsync({ id: userIdToDelete, }); setUserIdToDelete(""); fetchAllUsers.refetch(); } catch (error) { console.log(error); } }; //return an empty div return ( <div className="mx-auto p-8"> <div className="mb-8"> <h2 className="mb-4 text-2xl font-bold">Get All Users</h2>
 </div>
 <button className="rounded bg-blue-500 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-blue-600" onClick={() => fetchAllUsers.refetch()} > Get All Users </button>
 <div className="text- mb-4 mt-4 grid grid-cols-3 gap-4 font-bold"> <p>Id</p>
 <p>Name</p>
 <p>Email</p>
 </div>
 {fetchAllUsers.data && fetchAllUsers.data.map((user) => ( <div key={user.id} className="my-4 grid grid-cols-3 gap-4 rounded border border-gray-300 bg-white p-4 shadow" > <p>{user.id}</p>
 <p>{user.name}</p>
 <p>{user.email}</p>
 </div>
 ))} {/* Get one user UI */} <div className="mb-8"> <h2 className="mb-4 text-2xl font-bold">Get One User</h2>
 <div className="mb-4 flex"> <input className="mr-2 border border-gray-300 p-2" placeholder="Enter user id to get" value={userId || ""} onChange={(e) => setUserId(String(e.target.value))} />
 <button className="rounded bg-blue-500 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-blue-600" onClick={() => fetchOneUser.refetch()} > Get One User </button>
 </div>
 {fetchOneUser.data && ( <div> <p>Name: {fetchOneUser.data.name}</p>
 <p>Email: {fetchOneUser.data.email}</p>
 </div>
 )} </div>
 {/* Create User */} <div className="mb-8"> <h2 className="mb-4 text-2xl font-bold">Create New User</h2>
 <div className="mb-4 flex"> <input className="mr-2 w-1/2 border border-gray-300 p-2" placeholder="Name" value={name} onChange={(e) => setName(e.target.value)} />
 <input className="w-1/2 border border-gray-300 p-2" placeholder="Email" value={email} onChange={(e) => setEmail(e.target.value)} />
 </div>
 <button className="rounded bg-green-500 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-green-600" onClick={handleCreateUser} > Create User </button>
 </div>
 {/* Update User */} <div className="mb-8"> <h2 className="mb-4 text-2xl font-bold">Update User</h2>
 <div className="mb-4 flex"> <input className="mr-2 w-1/2 border border-gray-300 p-2" placeholder="Name to update" value={nameToUpdate} onChange={(e) => setNameToUpdate(e.target.value)} />
 <input className="w-1/2 border border-gray-300 p-2" placeholder="Email to update" value={emailToUpdate} onChange={(e) => setEmailToUpdate(e.target.value)} />
 </div>
 <input placeholder="Enter user id to update" className="mr-2 border border-gray-300 p-2" value={userIdToUpdate} onChange={(e) => setUserIdToUpdate(e.target.value)} />
 <button className="mt-2 rounded bg-orange-500 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-orange-600" onClick={handleUpdateUser} > Update User </button>
 </div>
 {/* Delete User */} <div className="mb-8"> <h2 className="mb-4 text-2xl font-bold">Delete User</h2>
 <input placeholder="Enter user id to delete" className="mr-2 border border-gray-300 p-2" value={userIdToDelete} onChange={(e) => setUserIdToDelete(e.target.value)} />
 <button className="mt-2 rounded bg-red-500 px-4 py-2 text-white hover:bg-red-600" onClick={handleDeleteUser} > Delete User </button>
 </div>
 </div>
 );
}

Explanation:

  • We create a single page application using Next.js, using the useRouter hook to get the query params from the URL.

  • We use the useMutation hook from react-query to create, update, and delete users.

  • We use the useQuery hook from react-query to fetch all users and fetch one user.

  • We have five functions to handle the CRUD operations:

  • getAllUsers - fetches all users

  • getOneUser - fetches one user

  • handleCreateUser - creates a new user

  • handleUpdateUser - updates a user

  • handleDeleteUser - deletes a user

We create a UI for each function styled with Tailwind CSS.

After each operation, we refetch the data to see the changes.

For example, after creating a new user, we refetch all users to see the new user in the list.

For a further explanation, check out the video: youtu.be/Gf9RkaHnsR8

Our final project should look like this:

This project is intended as an example so you can use it as a starting point for your projects. If you want to contribute, feel free to open a PR on GitHub (link in the video description).

__

🏁 Conclusion

We built a CRUD API using the following technologies:

  • Next.js
  • TypeScript
  • Tailwind CSS
  • tRPC
  • Prisma
  • Postgres
  • Docker

If you prefer a video version:

All the code is available for free on GitHub (link in video description).

You can connect with Francesco here

item.title: 设计天才：揭秘丹尼斯·里奇作为设计师的角色 | The Design Genius: Unveiling Dennis Ritchie's Role as a Designer
item.description:
短裙网03

发布于 7 月 4 日

丹尼斯·里奇 (Dennis Ritchie) 被誉为 C 编程语言的创建者和 Unix 操作系统的共同开发者，经常被誉为计算机科学和软件工程的先驱。 虽然他对该领域的贡献得到广泛认可，但他作为设计师的角色却经常被忽视。 在本文中，我们将深入研究 Ritchie 工作中鲜为人知的方面，并探讨为什么他不仅是一名程序员，而且还是一名出色的设计师。

设计艺术：

设计本质上涉及精心设计解决方案的过程，以解决特定问题或需求，同时优先考虑可用性、效率和美观。 它是关于寻找优雅而直观的方式来塑造技术。 尽管里奇主要因其编程能力而受到认可，但他在整个职业生涯中都表现出了非凡的设计敏感性。 以用户为中心的设计：**

在开发 C 编程语言时，Ritchie 专注于创建一种工具，使程序员能够编写高效且可移植的代码。 他仔细考虑了最终用户的需求和期望，确保该语言直观、灵活并支持模块化编程。 简洁优雅：**

里奇的设计以其简洁和优雅而闻名。 他相信“少即是多”的原则，并努力创建简洁、整洁的系统和编程语言。 建筑设计：**

除了编程语言之外，Ritchie 的设计技能还扩展到了架构方面的考虑。 作为 Unix 操作系统的共同创建者，他在该系统的设计和开发中发挥了关键作用。 这些设计决策使 Unix 变得灵活、健壮且易于扩展——这证明了 Ritchie 的架构能力。 协作设计：**

里奇的设计方法的特点是协作和开放。 他积极参与编程社区，寻求反馈并将建议纳入他的设计中。 这种协作精神在他开发 C 编程语言的工作中得到了体现，该语言受到了众多程序员的经验和见解的影响。 里奇倾听、迭代和根据反馈完善设计的能力对他们的成功发挥了至关重要的作用。 遗产和影响：**

丹尼斯·里奇作为设计师的贡献继续塑造着技术世界。 丹尼斯·里奇 (Dennis Ritchie) 不仅仅是一名程序员；他还是一名程序员。 他是一位富有远见的设计师，彻底改变了计算机科学和软件工程领域。 他对细节的一丝不苟、以用户为中心的方法、简单性和协作精神使他成为一名杰出的设计师。 当我们继续继承他的遗产时，有必要认识到 Ritchie 作为塑造现代计算基础的设计师的卓越贡献。

原文:

kiltwebment03

Posted on Jul 4

Dennis Ritchie, widely known as the creator of the C programming language and co-developer of the Unix operating system, is often hailed as a pioneer in computer science and software engineering. While his contributions to the field are widely acknowledged, his role as a designer is often overlooked. In this article, we will delve into the lesser-known aspect of Ritchie's work and explore why he was more than just a programmer but also a remarkable designer.

The Art of Design:

Design, in its essence, involves the thoughtful process of crafting solutions that address specific problems or needs while prioritizing usability, efficiency, and aesthetics. It is about finding elegant and intuitive ways to shape technology. Despite being primarily recognized for his programming prowess, Ritchie exhibited remarkable design sensibilities throughout his career. **

User-Centric Design:**

One of the defining characteristics of Ritchie's design approach was his commitment to user-centricity. While developing the C programming language, Ritchie focused on creating a tool that would empower programmers to write efficient and portable code. He carefully considered the needs and expectations of the end-users, ensuring that the language was intuitive, flexible, and supported modular programming. **

Simplicity and Elegance:**

Ritchie's designs were known for their simplicity and elegance. He believed in the principle of "less is more" and strived to create systems and programming languages that were concise and uncluttered. The C programming language exemplifies this philosophy, with its minimalist syntax and compact set of features. Ritchie understood that simplicity was key to fostering widespread adoption and facilitating effective communication among programmers. **

Architectural Design:**

Beyond programming languages, Ritchie's design skills extended to architectural considerations. As a co-creator of the Unix operating system, he played a pivotal role in its design and development. Unix introduced the concept of a modular and scalable operating system, with its emphasis on small, reusable utilities and a hierarchical file system. These design decisions enabled Unix to be flexible, robust, and easily extensible—a testament to Ritchie's architectural prowess. **

Collaborative Design:**

Ritchie's design approach was characterized by collaboration and openness. He actively engaged with the programming community, seeking feedback and incorporating suggestions into his designs. This collaborative spirit was evident in his work on the development of the C programming language, which was influenced by the experiences and insights of numerous programmers. Ritchie's ability to listen, iterate, and refine his designs based on feedback played a vital role in their success. **

Legacy and Impact:**

Dennis Ritchie's contributions as a designer continue to shape the world of technology. The C programming language, with its elegant design and versatility, remains one of the most widely used languages in software development. Unix, with its modular and scalable architecture, serves as the foundation for countless operating systems, including Linux and macOS. Ritchie's design philosophy has influenced subsequent generations of programmers, inspiring them to prioritize simplicity, usability, and user-centricity in their own creations.

Dennis Ritchie was more than just a programmer; he was a visionary designer who revolutionized the world of computer science and software engineering. His meticulous attention to detail, user-centric approach, simplicity, and collaborative spirit set him apart as an exceptional designer. The C programming language and Unix operating system stand as enduring testaments to his design genius. As we continue to build upon his legacy, it is essential to recognize Ritchie's remarkable contributions as a designer who shaped the foundations of modern computing.

item.title: 如何为您的网站选择合适的内容管理系统 (CMS) | How to Choose the Right Content Management System (CMS) for Your Website
item.description:
约翰逊约翰

发布于 7 月 4 日

选择正确的内容管理系统 (CMS) 对于有效的网站管理至关重要。 由于有众多可用选项，您可能会难以为您的业务做出最佳决策。 本分步指南将帮助您浏览选择过程并根据您的特定需求选择合适的 CMS。

第 1 步：评估您的要求

  • 确定您的网站目标、内容需求和未来的增长计划。
  • 确定您要创建的网站类型（博客、电子商务、作品集等）。
  • 考虑内容创建、用户管理、电子商务集成、SEO 工具和可扩展性等基本功能。

第 2 步：研究 CMS 选项

  • 探索不同的 CMS 平台，例如 WordPress、Joomla、Drupal、Shopify 等。
  • 阅读评论、比较功能并评估它们是否适合您的要求。
  • 考虑易用性、自定义选项、可用插件/扩展和社区支持等因素。

第 3 步：评估可扩展性和灵活性

  • 评估 CMS 处理未来增长和附加功能的能力。
  • 考虑 CMS 是否支持多站点管理、多种语言或移动响应能力。
  • 评估主题、模板和自定义选项的可用性以确保灵活性。

第 4 步：检查用户友好性

  • 测试 CMS 的用户界面和管理面板，确保其直观且易于导航。
  • 考虑您自己和您的团队成员的学习曲线。
  • 寻找拖放编辑器、可视化构建器和内容安排选项等功能。

第 5 步：审查安全措施

  • 检查 CMS 的安全功能和处理漏洞的跟踪记录。
  • 寻找定期更新、安全身份验证和用户权限控制等功能。
  • 考虑安全插件或扩展的可用性以提供额外的保护。

第 6 步：考虑支持和社区

  • 评估技术支持、文档和用户论坛的可用性。
  • 寻找一个活跃且乐于助人的社区来提供帮助和分享知识。
  • 检查专门从事所选 CMS 的专业服务或机构以获得额外支持。

第 7 步：评估成本和预算

  • 考虑 CMS 的成本，包括许可费用、托管费用和潜在的插件费用。
  • 比较定价计划并确定哪个选项符合您的预算和长期可持续性。

第 8 步：试驾

  • 安装 CMS 的演示版或试用版以获得实践经验。
  • 探索其功能、创建示例内容并测试自定义选项。
  • 评估其性能、速度和整体用户体验。

第 9 步：做出决定

  • 查看评估过程中收集的信息。
  • 优先考虑最能满足您的要求并符合您的目标的 CMS 选项。
  • 选择可提供功能、可用性、可扩展性、安全性、支持和成本的最佳组合的 CMS。

结论：
选择正确的 CMS 是实现有效网站管理的关键一步。 通过评估您的需求、研究选项、评估可扩展性、考虑用户友好性、审查安全措施和评估支持，您可以做出明智的决定。 按照此分步指南选择 CMS，使您能够为您的企业创建和管理成功的网站。

马特·安杰洛桑托 - 6 月 27 日

原文:

Johnson John

Posted on Jul 4

 

Choosing the right Content Management System (CMS) is crucial for effective website management. With numerous options available, it can be overwhelming to make the best decision for your business. This step-by-step guide will help you navigate the selection process and choose the right CMS based on your specific needs.

Step 1: Assess Your Requirements

  • Identify your website goals, content needs, and future growth plans.
  • Determine the type of website you want to create (blog, e-commerce, portfolio, etc.).
  • Consider essential features such as content creation, user management, e-commerce integration, SEO tools, and scalability.

Step 2: Research CMS Options

  • Explore different CMS platforms such as WordPress, Joomla, Drupal, Shopify, and others.
  • Read reviews, compare features, and evaluate their suitability for your requirements.
  • Consider factors like ease of use, customization options, available plugins/extensions, and community support.

Step 3: Evaluate Scalability and Flexibility

  • Assess the CMS's ability to handle future growth and additional functionality.
  • Consider whether the CMS supports multi-site management, multiple languages, or mobile responsiveness.
  • Evaluate the availability of themes, templates, and customization options to ensure flexibility.

Step 4: Check User-Friendliness

  • Test the CMS's user interface and admin panel to ensure it is intuitive and easy to navigate.
  • Consider the learning curve for yourself and your team members.
  • Look for features like drag-and-drop editors, visual builders, and content scheduling options.

Step 5: Review Security Measures

  • Examine the CMS's security features and track record for handling vulnerabilities.
  • Look for features like regular updates, secure authentication, and user permission controls.
  • Consider the availability of security plugins or extensions for added protection.

Step 6: Consider Support and Community

  • Assess the availability of technical support, documentation, and user forums.
  • Look for an active and helpful community that can provide assistance and share knowledge.
  • Check for professional services or agencies that specialize in the chosen CMS for additional support.

Step 7: Evaluate Cost and Budget

  • Consider the cost of the CMS, including licensing fees, hosting, and potential plugin expenses.
  • Compare the pricing plans and determine which option aligns with your budget and long-term sustainability.

Step 8: Take a Test Drive

  • Install a demo or trial version of the CMS to get hands-on experience.
  • Explore its features, create sample content, and test customization options.
  • Evaluate its performance, speed, and overall user experience.

Step 9: Make Your Decision

  • Review the information gathered during the evaluation process.
  • Prioritize the CMS options that best meet your requirements and align with your goals.
  • Select the CMS that offers the best combination of features, usability, scalability, security, support, and cost.

Conclusion:
Choosing the right CMS is a crucial step towards effective website management. By assessing your requirements, researching options, evaluating scalability, considering user-friendliness, reviewing security measures, and assessing support, you can make an informed decision. Follow this step-by-step guide to select the CMS that will empower you to create and manage a successful website for your business.

Matt Angelosanto - Jun 27
